summary: Eclectic Boston set highlighted by a soaring Truckin' → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia
blurb: The show builds from tight early numbers into an electrifying second‑set medley, with the Truckin’ → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia sequence stealing the spotlight. Keith’s keyboards rise to prominence and Jerry delivers strong lead work despite a rough China Cat intro. Overall a high‑energy performance worth revisiting.
review: On September 15, 1972 at Boston Music Hall the Dead delivered an eclectic, heavy‑hitter set that ramps up into one of the season’s most memorable second‑set stretches. After a solid first set – highlighted by Playin' In The Band and a brief stumble on China Cat Sunflower’s intro – the band launches into a blistering Truckin’ → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia sequence, with Keith Godchaux’s Moog‑filled fills and an inspired solo that lift the whole jam. Jerry Garcia’s lead work is strong throughout, especially in the soaring peaks of the second set, while the rhythm section locks in tight grooves despite early monitor glitches. Fans will appreciate the crisp soundboard capture that preserves the band’s dynamic flow, making this show a must‑listen for anyone craving classic ’72 energy.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings give glowing positive remarks about the Truckin' → The Other One sequence, Keith's keyboards and overall performance; two additional recordings are mixed but focus on recording flaws rather than musical shortcomings, reinforcing a net positive sentiment.

best_recording.reason: Full‑show soundboard capture with crisp clarity, only minor early mix issues; includes the standout Truckin' → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia sequence.

key_highlights: Stellar Truckin' → The Other One → Sugar Magnolia medley
Keith Godchaux’s Moog textures and solo on The Other One
High‑energy second set that lifts the entire concert
Overall eclectic setlist with strong tight‑hitting first half

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s lead work is strong throughout, with only a slightly loose tempo on the China Cat Sunflower intro.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provides solid rhythm support; his guitar tone sounds a bit thin early but steadies later in the set.
band_performance.Keith: Keith’s keyboards sit low early but burst into vibrant Moog textures, delivering syncopated fills and an inspired solo on The Other One.
